name: Stripe
problem: Online payments were too complex for developers — required integrating multiple banking partners, handling compliance, and writing thousands of lines of boilerplate code
solution: 7 lines of code to accept payments globally. Developer-first payment APIs that abstract away all banking complexity
traction: $2M ARR, thousands of businesses processing payments, growing 20% month-over-month at Series A
market: Global payments, $2T+ annual volume, every internet business needs this
founder_signal: Patrick and John Collison, technical founders, prev YC W09, obsessive about developer experience
sequoia_thesis: Developer infrastructure play with compounding network effects — every developer who learns Stripe becomes an advocate. High technical moat in compliance, banking relationships, and fraud detection. Category-defining API company.

name: Airbnb
problem: Hotels are expensive and impersonal; millions of homes and spare rooms sit empty while travelers overpay for generic experiences
solution: Peer-to-peer marketplace for short-term home rentals — turns idle home equity into income for hosts, unique stays for guests
traction: $1M ARR, 10,000+ listings across 100+ cities, supply growing 3x per month, strong NPS on both sides
market: Global travel & hospitality, $500B+ market dominated by incumbents with high fixed costs
founder_signal: Brian Chesky, Joe Gebbia, Nathan Blecharczyk — design-led founders who hustled first customers manually, incredible storytellers
sequoia_thesis: Asset-light marketplace with powerful supply-demand network effects. Category creation in alternative accommodations. Founders with missionary zeal who manually scaled supply in each city.

name: DoorDash
problem: Local restaurants had no delivery infrastructure — losing customers to chains that could afford logistics. Consumers wanted restaurant delivery but options were limited to pizza
solution: On-demand food delivery marketplace connecting consumers, restaurants, and dashers. Full-stack logistics layer for local commerce
traction: $300K MRR, operating in 10+ cities, strong restaurant retention, growing order frequency per user
market: US food delivery $200B+, local commerce logistics $1T+ globally
founder_signal: Tony Xu, Stanley Tang, Andy Fang, Evan Moore — Stanford students who literally did deliveries themselves to understand operations
sequoia_thesis: Winner-take-most logistics marketplace with supply density network effects. Founders with operational obsession who understand unit economics at block level. Path to owning local commerce infrastructure.

name: Zoom
problem: Video conferencing was broken — Cisco WebEx and Microsoft Lync were unreliable, hard to use, and required IT setup. Remote meetings were painful
solution: Video-first communications platform that just works — no downloads required for guests, HD video, simple joining experience
traction: $6M ARR at Series B, 400+ enterprise customers, 40% month-over-month growth, NPS of 70+ (vs competitors at <20)
market: Enterprise communications $43B, every knowledge worker globally needs this
founder_signal: Eric Yuan, ex-Cisco/WebEx engineering VP — built WebEx from 10 to 15,000 engineers, left to fix what he knew was broken
sequoia_thesis: Category replacement in video communications led by the engineer who built the incumbent. Viral product-led growth where every meeting invites new users. Infrastructure moat in reliability and global data center optimization.

name: Snowflake
problem: Cloud data warehouses forced customers to choose between performance and cost. On-premise solutions like Teradata were expensive; cloud alternatives were slow or required DBAs to tune constantly
solution: Cloud-native data warehouse that separates compute from storage — scale each independently, pay only for what you use, zero administration
traction: $2M ARR, 80 enterprise customers, 150% net revenue retention — customers expand massively after initial deployment
market: Enterprise data warehouse $15B growing to $100B+ as all data moves to cloud
founder_signal: Benoit Dageville, Thierry Cruanes, Marcin Zukowski — database PhDs and engineers from Oracle who spent careers on this exact problem
sequoia_thesis: Data platform with consumption-based model creating extreme land-and-expand dynamics. Network effects through data sharing marketplace. Deep technical moat in multi-cluster shared data architecture.

name: MongoDB
problem: Relational databases like Oracle and MySQL forced developers to model data in rigid tables — painful for modern apps with flexible, hierarchical data structures
solution: Document-oriented database that stores data as flexible JSON-like documents. Matches how developers think about their data
traction: $8M ARR, 1,000+ paying customers, millions of free users creating strong pipeline, 20% quarter-over-quarter growth
market: Database market $50B+, every application needs a database, developer preference drives enterprise adoption
founder_signal: Dwight Merriman, Eliot Horowitz — founders of DoubleClick (sold to Google for $3.1B), built MongoDB to solve their own scaling pain
sequoia_thesis: Developer-first database riding shift from SQL to NoSQL. Free tier creates massive organic adoption that converts to enterprise. Deep open-source community creates defensibility against commercial alternatives.

name: Klarna
problem: Online checkout abandonment was 70%+ because consumers didn't want to enter credit card details or pay full price upfront for items they weren't sure about
solution: Buy now, pay later at checkout — one-click purchase experience with flexible payment options. Merchants pay a fee; consumers get 0% financing
traction: $5M ARR, 1,000+ merchants integrated, 500K consumers, Sweden's #1 payment method at many e-commerce sites
market: Global e-commerce payments $5T+, consumer credit $1T+, massive incumbent displacement opportunity
founder_signal: Sebastian Siemiatkowski, Niklas Adalberth, Victor Jacobsson — Stockholm School of Economics founders with deep consumer finance insight
sequoia_thesis: Payments network that aligns merchant incentives (higher conversion) with consumer incentives (flexible payments). Two-sided network effects — more merchants drive consumer adoption and vice versa. Category creation in BNPL.

name: Instacart
problem: Grocery shopping consumes 1–2 hours per week per household. Grocers have no delivery capability; consumers want convenience but online grocery had failed repeatedly
solution: On-demand grocery delivery in 1 hour via personal shoppers — asset-light model partnering with existing grocery chains
traction: $1M MRR, operating in 5 cities, 60% repeat purchase rate within 30 days, grocers actively partnering (not just tolerating)
market: US grocery $700B, only 3% online vs 15%+ in other retail — massive structural shift underway
founder_signal: Apoorva Mehta, 20 failed startup ideas before this, ex-Amazon supply chain engineer who understood logistics deeply
sequoia_thesis: Last-mile grocery logistics marketplace with supply-side density moats in each market. Network effects as shoppers specialize in specific stores. Strategic value to grocers who have no digital capability.

name: Plaid
problem: Fintech apps needed to connect to users' bank accounts to work, but bank APIs were nonexistent — developers had to use brittle screen-scraping that broke constantly
solution: Universal API layer for bank connectivity — one integration to access account data from 11,000+ financial institutions
traction: $3M ARR, 500+ fintech developers integrated, processing millions of connections, Venmo and Robinhood as reference customers
market: Every fintech app needs bank connectivity — $30B+ developer infrastructure opportunity as financial services move to API-first
founder_signal: Zach Perret, William Hockey — ex-Bain consultants who turned down Goldman offers to solve fintech infrastructure, obsessive about developer experience
sequoia_thesis: Financial data infrastructure with near-monopoly network effects — the more fintechs use Plaid, the more banks prioritize supporting it. High switching costs once integrated. Inevitable as all financial services go digital.

name: Robinhood
problem: $7–$10 per trade commissions put retail investing out of reach for young, low-balance investors. Incumbent brokerages designed for wealthy customers
solution: Commission-free stock trading app with consumer-grade UX — mobile-first, no minimums, instant account opening
traction: 1M+ person waitlist before launch, 100K+ funded accounts in first month, 90% under 30 years old
market: US retail brokerage $35B revenue market. 200M Americans with no investment account — massive greenfield
founder_signal: Vlad Tenev, Baiju Bhatt — Stanford roommates, built HFT infrastructure for hedge funds, understood market mechanics deeply
sequoia_thesis: Category disruption via radical price reduction (zero commissions) creates mass market. Viral growth through referrals. Data and order flow economics enable profitability at scale. Generational brand for new investors.

name: WhatsApp
problem: International SMS was expensive ($0.10–$0.25 per message). Carrier messaging was slow, unreliable, and had no group functionality
solution: Free messaging over data connection — cross-platform, minimal design, no ads, no games. Just messaging that works
traction: 250M monthly active users, 1B+ messages/day, growing 1M users/day, near-zero churn once contacts are on platform
market: Global messaging — 5B+ mobile phone users, universal behavior, carrier messaging is a $100B revenue stream ripe for disruption
founder_signal: Jan Koum, Brian Acton — ex-Yahoo engineers, Jan grew up in Soviet Ukraine understanding privacy's value, refused VC ads pressure for years
sequoia_thesis: Pure network effects — value increases with every user added. Extreme product discipline (no ads ever) creates trust moat. International-first strategy captures markets US companies ignored.

name: YouTube
problem: Video content was trapped on hard drives and DVDs. Sharing video required massive files, technical knowledge, and proprietary formats. TV was the only distribution
solution: Universal video hosting and sharing — upload once, watch anywhere, embeddable everywhere. Handles transcoding automatically
traction: 8M video views/day growing 100% monthly, 65,000 uploads/day, becoming default embed for blogs and news sites
market: Video entertainment $150B TV advertising market, creator economy nascent but massive
founder_signal: Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, Jawed Karim — ex-PayPal engineers who understood viral mechanics and scaling infrastructure
sequoia_thesis: User-generated video platform with content supply and demand network effects. Every video uploaded draws viewers; viewer base draws creators. Infrastructure moat in global video delivery. Inevitable acquisition target for platform players.

name: Dropbox
problem: File sync across devices and backup was painful — USB drives, emailing files to yourself, and manual FTP were the alternatives. Losing files was common
solution: Folder on your computer that syncs magically to all devices and the cloud. Invisible infrastructure that just works
traction: 100K beta users from one HN post, growing 3,900% in 15 months after referral launch, 70%+ weekly active usage
market: Every computer user globally — 500M+ knowledge workers, expanding to teams and enterprise
founder_signal: Drew Houston, Arash Ferdowsi — MIT engineers, Drew built it because he kept forgetting his USB drive, missionary passion for simplicity
sequoia_thesis: Consumer viral growth converting to enterprise land-and-expand. File sync creates daily habit and extreme retention. Referral economics with negative net CAC. Path from personal storage to team collaboration.

name: Meraki (Cisco)
problem: Enterprise networking (WiFi, switches, firewalls) required expensive on-premise hardware AND expensive IT staff to configure and manage it. SMBs couldn't afford either
solution: Cloud-managed networking hardware — enterprise-grade WiFi and switches managed entirely through a browser dashboard with zero on-site IT
traction: $4M ARR, 500+ enterprise customers, hardware + SaaS hybrid with 75%+ gross margins on software component
market: Enterprise networking $30B+ market, SMB and mid-market totally underserved by Cisco and Juniper
founder_signal: Sanjit Biswas, John Bicket, Hans Robertson — MIT CSAIL PhD students who built mesh networking for One Laptop per Child
sequoia_thesis: Cloud-first networking disrupts on-premise incumbents via price and simplicity. SaaS model on hardware creates recurring revenue with high switching costs. Acquisition target for networking giants lacking cloud management layer.

name: ServiceNow
problem: IT service management ran on email and spreadsheets or legacy on-premise software (Remedy) that cost millions and took years to implement. IT teams couldn't measure or improve their performance
solution: Cloud-based IT service management platform — ticketing, asset management, workflows, all in one modern SaaS application
traction: $15M ARR, 200+ enterprise customers including Fortune 500, 130% net revenue retention, replacing Remedy at major accounts
market: IT operations software $25B, expanding to HR, legal, facilities — any workflow-heavy enterprise department
founder_signal: Fred Luddy, former CTO of Peregrine Systems — spent 20 years in ITSM, built ServiceNow to fix everything he knew was broken
sequoia_thesis: Workflow platform starting with IT and expanding horizontally across every enterprise department. Deep integration creates high switching costs. Negative churn as customers add modules. Category-defining enterprise platform.

name: Nubank
problem: Brazilian banking was an oligopoly of 5 banks charging 300%+ APR on credit cards, with terrible UX, massive fees, and customer service designed to frustrate
solution: Digital-first credit card with no fees, mobile app with full control, transparent pricing, and human customer service
traction: 500K waitlist before launch, 100K customers in first 6 months, NPS of 87 vs -1 for incumbents, 40% of customers came via referrals
market: Brazilian financial services $150B revenue, 60M unbanked or underbanked Brazilians, expanding across Latin America
founder_signal: David Vélez, Cristina Junqueira, Edward Wible — ex-Goldman, ex-Itaú bank executives who deeply understood where incumbents were extracting value
sequoia_thesis: Digital banking disruption in captured market with zero competitive response capability from incumbents (regulatory capture). NPS-driven viral growth with near-zero CAC. Expanding product surface from credit card to full financial OS.

name: Figma
problem: Design tools like Sketch were desktop-only and single-player — collaborating on designs required emailing files, commenting on screenshots, and endless version confusion
solution: Browser-based collaborative design tool — multiple people in the same file in real-time, like Google Docs for design
traction: $3M ARR, 150K designers, 80% retention at 12 months, viral growth as teams share files with stakeholders who then sign up
market: Design tools $5B growing to $30B+ as design becomes core to every product company's competitive advantage
founder_signal: Dylan Field, Evan Wallace — MIT and Brown engineers who spent 4 years building a WebGL rendering engine from scratch before launch
sequoia_thesis: Collaboration multiplier that grows with team size — every designer who uses Figma becomes a distribution channel. Technical moat in browser-based rendering performance. Network effects as files shared externally convert new users.

name: Unity Technologies
problem: Building 3D games and interactive experiences required teams of 50+ engineers and millions in engine licensing fees — only large studios could create quality content
solution: Democratized game engine — professional-grade 3D development tools accessible to indie developers and small studios, with cross-platform deployment
traction: $20M ARR, 500K registered developers, powering 50%+ of mobile games on App Store, growing 60% year-over-year
market: Game development tools $3B, but underlying opportunity is all interactive 3D experiences — $150B+ in games, simulation, architecture, film
founder_signal: David Helgason, Nicholas Francis, Joachim Ante — Danish indie game developers who built the engine for themselves after struggling with existing tools
sequoia_thesis: Creator platform with flywheel: more developers → more content → more players → more monetization → more developers. Asset store creates marketplace network effects. Expanding beyond games to enterprise simulation and AR/VR infrastructure.

name: Cohesity
problem: Enterprise data management was fragmented across dozens of backup, archive, test/dev, and analytics point solutions — creating 'dark data' sprawl and massive operational cost
solution: Hyperconverged secondary storage — single platform consolidating backup, archive, files, and analytics on one scale-out system
traction: $5M ARR, 50 enterprise customers (average $100K+ ACV), 90% gross margins, expanding to backup + analytics within same accounts
market: Secondary storage market $10B+, data management software $30B — every large enterprise overspends on redundant data infrastructure
founder_signal: Mohit Aron — co-founder and original architect of Google File System, then founder of Nutanix (took to $1B+ revenue), solving storage at third major company
sequoia_thesis: Category consolidation play in enterprise storage led by the person who literally designed Google's distributed file system. High technical moat. Recurring revenue with extreme enterprise stickiness.

name: PagerDuty
problem: When production systems went down, on-call engineers had no reliable way to be alerted — SMS was unreliable, email was too slow, phone trees were manual and error-prone
solution: Incident management and on-call alerting platform — routes alerts from any monitoring tool to the right engineer via smart escalation policies
traction: $2M ARR, 500+ customers including Yelp and Etsy, 120%+ net revenue retention as teams add more services and users
market: IT operations management $15B, every DevOps team globally needs incident response infrastructure
founder_signal: Andrew Miklas, Baskar Puvanathasan, Alex Solomon — ex-Amazon AWS engineers who built alerting infrastructure internally and saw the gap for everyone else
sequoia_thesis: Critical infrastructure for DevOps — high switching cost once pager policies and escalation rules are configured. Viral adoption as companies grow their engineering teams. Expands from alerting to full incident management platform.

name: Okta
problem: As enterprises adopted more SaaS apps (Salesforce, Workday, Box), managing separate logins for each became a security and productivity nightmare. IT had no visibility or control
solution: Identity-as-a-service — single sign-on for all enterprise apps, multi-factor authentication, and lifecycle management from one cloud dashboard
traction: $3M ARR, 150 enterprise customers, 125% net revenue retention as customers add users and integrate more apps
market: Identity and access management $8B growing as SaaS adoption accelerates — every employee at every company needs this
founder_signal: Todd McKinnon, Frederic Kerrest — ex-Salesforce SVP of Engineering (Todd built Salesforce from 300 to 3,000 engineers) and ex-Salesforce business development
sequoia_thesis: Identity infrastructure that becomes more valuable as SaaS adoption grows — Okta benefits from every new SaaS app an enterprise adopts. Network effects through app integrations catalog. Highest-priority security infrastructure.

name: Natera
problem: Prenatal genetic testing required invasive amniocentesis with 1-in-200 miscarriage risk — most pregnant women couldn't safely access comprehensive genetic screening
solution: Non-invasive prenatal testing via simple blood draw — analyzes fetal DNA fragments in maternal blood to screen for chromosomal abnormalities with >99% accuracy
traction: $25M ARR, 200,000+ tests performed, reimbursement secured from major insurers, growing 80% year-over-year
market: 4M births/year in US alone, prenatal testing $1B growing to $3B+; expanding to oncology liquid biopsy $10B+
founder_signal: Matthew Rabinowitz, Styrmir Sigurdsson — bioinformatics PhDs who published foundational research, 7+ years of scientific development before commercialization
sequoia_thesis: Proprietary bioinformatics algorithms create deep technical moat in a highly regulated market with high barriers to entry. Sticky reimbursement relationships. Platform technology expanding from prenatal to cancer detection.

name: Veeva Systems
problem: Life sciences companies ran on Salesforce + massive customizations or legacy on-premise CRM — costing $50M+ to implement and requiring armies of consultants to maintain
solution: Industry-specific cloud software for pharma and biotech — CRM, content management, and data built specifically for life sciences regulatory and commercial needs
traction: $10M ARR, 50 pharma customers including top 20 global pharma, 140% net revenue retention, replacing Salesforce at major accounts
market: Life sciences IT spending $15B annually — every pharma and biotech company globally needs commercial and regulatory software
founder_signal: Peter Gassner — VP of Technology at Salesforce who saw the vertical opportunity, 25 years in enterprise software including PeopleSoft, built Veeva with deep industry insight
sequoia_thesis: Vertical SaaS with regulatory-grade compliance moat — pharma companies cannot switch vendors easily due to FDA audit trails. Industry-specific data creates ecosystem lock-in. Founder knows both the technology and the industry.

name: HashiCorp
problem: DevOps teams managing multi-cloud infrastructure had no consistent way to provision, secure, and manage servers, networks, and secrets across AWS, Azure, and GCP
solution: Open-source infrastructure automation tools — Terraform (provisioning), Vault (secrets), Consul (networking), Nomad (workload management)
traction: $5M ARR, 3M+ Terraform downloads, 50K+ active open-source contributors, enterprise commercial adoption accelerating among companies with 500+ engineers
market: Cloud infrastructure management $30B+ as multi-cloud becomes standard. Every large engineering team has this problem
founder_signal: Mitchell Hashimoto, Armon Dadgar — core infrastructure engineers who built the tools for themselves, Terraform became de facto standard before commercial launch
sequoia_thesis: Open-source flywheel — millions of free users create awareness, skills, and community that convert to enterprise. Terraform's dominance creates lock-in through HCL codebase investment. Multi-product platform across the DevOps workflow.

name: Guardant Health
problem: Cancer is most treatable when caught early, but screening requires invasive biopsies — limiting who gets tested and how often. Tumor biopsies also miss heterogeneity within a tumor
solution: Liquid biopsy blood tests that detect cancer DNA fragments — Guardant360 guides treatment for advanced cancer; Shield screens for colorectal cancer from a blood draw
traction: $40M ARR, 200+ biopharma partnerships, covered by major insurers for advanced cancer, FDA breakthrough designation for Shield
market: Cancer diagnostics $15B, liquid biopsy potentially replacing $50B+ of traditional biopsy and imaging in long-term
founder_signal: Helmy Eltoukhy, AmirAli Talasaz — Stanford electrical engineering PhDs who applied signal processing to genomics, 5 years of scientific foundation before launch
sequoia_thesis: Platform technology with defensible IP across cancer detection and monitoring. Regulatory moats create 3–5 year competitive advantage windows. Recurring testing model as cancer becomes manageable chronic condition.

name: Faire
problem: Independent retailers couldn't risk buying wholesale inventory they didn't know would sell — minimum orders were $500-$5,000+ with no returns. Small brands had no way to reach retailers at scale
solution: B2B wholesale marketplace with net-60 payment terms and free returns — retailers can try new brands risk-free; brands get distribution without a salesforce
traction: $5M GMV/month, 5,000+ retailers, 500+ brands, 70% retailer retention month-over-month, growing 25% month-over-month
market: US wholesale $1.5T+, independent retail $1T globally — entirely un-digitized, running on trade shows and cold calls
founder_signal: Max Rhodes, Daniyar Naimanbayev, Marcelo Cortes, Jeff Kolovson — ex-Square leaders who understood SMB merchant dynamics and payment risk models
sequoia_thesis: Two-sided marketplace with financial innovation (net-60 terms, free returns) that fundamentally changes risk dynamics for both sides. Supply-demand flywheel accelerates with each city and category. Data advantage improves inventory recommendations over time.

name: Samsara
problem: Industrial companies (trucking, construction, manufacturing) were running blind — no real-time visibility into fleet location, driver safety, equipment health, or operational efficiency
solution: IoT platform for physical operations — GPS tracking, dashcams, sensor data, and workflow software in a single cloud platform for industrial companies
traction: $20M ARR, 500+ enterprise customers, 145% net revenue retention as customers deploy more sensors and expand to new workflows
market: Industrial IoT $200B+, fleet management alone $20B — every company with physical operations globally is a prospect
founder_signal: Sanjit Biswas and John Bicket — same founders who built Meraki (sold to Cisco for $1.2B), pattern-matching on building hardware+software platforms for operational teams
sequoia_thesis: Platform company in the physical economy with hardware-enabled data moat. Each sensor deployed generates proprietary operational data. Expansion from fleet to all physical operations creates massive platform opportunity.

name: Notion
problem: Knowledge workers used 5+ different tools for docs, wikis, tasks, and spreadsheets — each siloed, requiring constant context switching, with no single source of truth for team knowledge
solution: All-in-one workspace — docs, databases, kanban, wikis, and tasks built on a flexible block-based editor that lets teams build custom workflows
traction: $5M ARR, 500K users, 70% week-over-week retention for activated users, viral team growth as one member invites the whole company
market: Productivity software $50B+, knowledge management $10B — every knowledge worker globally is a prospect
founder_signal: Ivan Zhao, Simon Last — founders who spent 2 years rebuilding from scratch after nearly shutting down, obsessive product vision for 10+ years
sequoia_thesis: Horizontal productivity platform with network effects — each team member who adopts creates more value for teammates and pulls in new users. Highly viral PLG with enterprise expansion. Sticky because teams encode their entire workflow in Notion.

name: OpenAI (early)
problem: AI research was concentrated in a few large tech companies, with safety research underfunded and capabilities advancing without adequate alignment work
solution: Safety-focused AI research lab pursuing AGI for the benefit of humanity — open publication of research, development of foundation models
traction: GPT-2 and GPT-3 scientific breakthroughs with massive organic developer adoption; 10B+ API calls before commercial launch; ChatGPT reached 100M users in 60 days
market: AI is a general-purpose technology — infrastructure layer for every industry globally, $15T+ GDP impact potential
founder_signal: Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, Elon Musk — combination of top AI researchers and proven operators, Ilya co-invented modern deep learning at Google Brain
sequoia_thesis: Foundation model platform that could be the most consequential technology company ever built. Data and compute flywheel. Every new capability creates new markets. Winner-take-most dynamics in AI infrastructure.
